title: Making a two-volume slipcase
subtitle: A board-and-cloth drop-spine case for two matching books
excerpt: A slipcase holds one or two books by enclosing five of their six faces, leaving the spine exposed for reading the title and easy removal. This tutorial builds a drop-spine slipcase for two side-by-side volumes in book cloth, with mitred corners and an optional ribbon pull. No sewing is involved.
sourceNotes: Slipcase geometry is described in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), Internet Archive, in the context of portfolio and case construction. The five-board drop-spine slipcase structure is synthesised from general bookbinding geometry; Arthur Johnson, The Thames and Hudson Manual of Bookbinding (1978) contains structural detail but is in copyright: structural description only is drawn here.

glossaryTerms.definition: A protective case that fits around a book or set of books, covering five faces and leaving the spine open for reading the title and for pulling the book out. A drop-spine slipcase opens at the top; a solander box has a hinged lid. These are different structures.

The direction in which most paper or board fibres run. For a slipcase, all boards must be cut with grain parallel to the spine of the books inside. Board cut against the grain is less rigid and may buckle when glued.

A 45° corner cut in the covering cloth that allows the turn-in to fold flat at each corner without a lump of excess material. A clean mitre leaves approximately 1.5 times the board thickness of cloth at the very tip of each corner.

projectSchedule.title: Measure, cut boards, and cover
projectSchedule.body: Measure both volumes carefully. Cut the five boards. Glue and cover in book cloth with mitred corners. Press under weights.

projectSchedule.title: Assemble the slipcase
projectSchedule.body: Boards have dried overnight. Glue the five panels together in sequence: base first, then the two sides, then the back, then the top. Leave to cure under weight.

content.text: A
slipcase

is a five-faced box open at the spine end, built to exactly the measurements of the books it holds. This tutorial makes a drop-spine slipcase (open at the top) wide enough for two volumes placed side by side. The five panels are the back, the two sides, the base, and the top. They are covered in book cloth before assembly and glued with PVA at each joint. No sewing is needed. Measuring accurately is the critical skill: a slipcase that is 2 mm too wide lets the books rattle; one that is 2 mm too narrow will not close.

items.name: Bookbinding board, 3 mm (for all five panel faces)
items.qty: enough for the measured dimensions of all five panels

items.name: Book cloth for covering
items.qty: enough to cover each panel with a 20 mm turn-in on all edges

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm for inner lining (optional)
items.qty: cut to the inner faces of each panel if lining is desired

items.name: Ribbon, 10 mm wide (optional, for pull tab at base)
items.qty: one length, twice the interior depth of the slipcase

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones: blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler held firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, never in front of it. Bookbinding adhesives: PVA is safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Clean brushes with warm water immediately after use.

content.text: Measuring the books

A slipcase measurement uses five dimensions from the books: head-to-tail height (H), spine-to-foredge depth (D), spine thickness of each volume (S), and the combined width of both volumes side by side (W = spine width of vol 1 + spine width of vol 2 + any gap). Measure each of these five times and take the largest reading. Books are rarely perfectly rectangular.
Add 1 mm to every dimension for clearance before adding the board thickness to each panel calculation. A slipcase built to the exact book dimensions will not close comfortably.
Calculating the five boards
The five panels interlock like a shallow box. Each board dimension accounts for the thickness of the boards it butts against. Using T = 3 mm board thickness:
Back panel: H × W (full height × full combined width)
Two side panels: (D − T) × H (depth minus back thickness × full height)
Base panel: (D − T) × (W − 2T) (depth minus back × width minus both sides)
Top panel: same dimensions as the base panel
Cut all five boards with the
grain direction

parallel to the spine of the housed books on every board. For the back and sides, this means the grain runs head-to-tail. For the base and top, the grain runs from the spine end toward the open end (front to back of the slipcase). A slipcase with a single board cut against grain will cup and distort the joint.
Covering the panels

Cover each board individually before assembly. Cut the book cloth 40 mm wider and taller than the board to allow a 20 mm turn-in on each edge. Apply PVA thinly and evenly to the board face with the paste brush. Press the board onto the cloth, centred. Flip and rub down with the bone folder.
Cut the
mitres

at each corner, leaving a small triangle of cloth at the board corner tip. Fold the turn-ins, head and tail first, then sides. Rub each firmly with the bone folder. Press each covered panel under a flat weight for at least 30 minutes before assembly. A damp board that curves at the turn-ins has not been pressed enough.
Assembly sequence

Glue the base panel to the back panel first. Run a line of PVA along the bottom edge of the back panel's inside face and butt the base panel against it at 90°. Hold or clamp for five minutes. Then glue the two side panels to the back and base, one at a time. Finally, glue the top panel to complete the box. Check the interior dimensions at each step with the actual books: the books should slide in with light resistance, not rattle.

If a ribbon pull is included, fold a length of ribbon in half and glue it to the inside face of the base panel, centred, before gluing the base in place. The doubled ribbon forms a loop at the floor of the slipcase; the books rest on top of it. Pulling the ribbon loop lifts the books out without having to grip the spines.
Finishing

The slipcase can be lined on its interior faces with a plain or complementary paper. Cut the lining paper slightly smaller than each interior face (about 2 mm smaller on each edge) and paste in with diluted PVA. This improves the feel and covers any rough board edges inside the case.
Leave the assembled slipcase to cure under weight for at least a day before inserting the books. PVA joints reach working strength quickly but full cure takes longer under the load of two books.
Care

Store the slipcase upright. The open spine end should face outward on the shelf so the books are visible by their spines. Do not store a slipcase lying flat with the books inside: the weight can bow the base panel over time. Book cloth cleans with a dry, barely damp cloth; never saturate the cloth surface.
Variations

A single-volume slipcase follows exactly the same construction: simply use the spine width of one book for W and omit the second volume from the measurement. A slipcase for a very tall or wide book uses 4 mm or 5 mm board rather than 3 mm to avoid racking.
